name: airport_runway_wildlife_radar_shutdown
premise: An airport's wildlife radar detects repeated bird flocks near an active runway during peak departures. Maintenance chief Sara Ali says the radar is producing intermittent false positives after a lightning strike and recommends shutting it down for recalibration, which would force slower manual spotting and more delays. Air traffic lead Ben Wallace argues that keeping a degraded system online may create complacency and confusion. The airline alliance threatens penalties if delays continue, and a recent bird strike made national news. The recalibration will take six hours; a storm front arrives in three hours and will reduce visibility. External scrutiny from regulators and community observers has intensified because the outcome could redefine expectations in Aviation Safety.

agents.name: Ben Wallace
agents.identity: I am the air traffic lead responsible for runway safety decisions when imperfect detection systems can either prevent or cause dangerous mistakes.
agents.goal: I want a clear operational picture that reduces collision risk without relying on signals we cannot trust under deteriorating weather.

agents.name: Sara Ali
agents.identity: I am the maintenance chief responsible for equipment integrity when running degraded systems can produce false confidence and legal exposure.
agents.goal: I want systems either trustworthy or offline, because half-working safety tools can be worse than none.

choices: I will shut down the radar for recalibration and run conservative manual spotting with increased spacing, accepting delays to restore a trustworthy baseline.
I will keep the radar online with a clear degraded-status protocol and cross-checks, accepting confusion risk to preserve some detection capability during low visibility.
forced_choice.call_to_action: Visibility is about to drop and the radar is unreliable. Ben Wallace must choose between shutting it down for recalibration or keeping it online with degraded protocols. What does Ben Wallace do?
